questions.questionText: Vancomycin is the drug of choice for:
questions.optionB: MRSA (methicillin-resistant S. aureus) and C. difficile colitis (oral vancomycin)
questions.explanation: Vancomycin: glycopeptide antibiotic, inhibits cell wall synthesis (binds D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursor → blocks transglycosylation). Bactericidal for most organisms. Drug of choice for MRSA (IV) and C. difficile (oral — not absorbed systemically). Toxicities: 'Red man syndrome' (histamine release from rapid infusion — NOT allergy/anaphylaxis, prevented by slow infusion), nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity. Resistance: VRE (vancomycin-resistant Enterococci — D-Ala-D-Lac). Reference: KD Tripathi — Vancomycin.

questions.questionText: Metronidazole is the drug of choice for:
questions.optionB: Anaerobic bacterial infections and protozoal infections (Giardia, Entamoeba, Trichomonas)
questions.explanation: Metronidazole: nitroimidazole — enters anaerobic organisms → reduced by ferredoxin → forms cytotoxic free radicals → DNA damage. Bactericidal for ANAEROBES only (B. fragilis), also effective against: Giardia, E. histolytica, T. vaginalis, C. difficile (oral, alternative). Disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol (avoid alcohol). Other uses: H. pylori triple therapy, brain abscess (crosses BBB), liver abscess (amebic). Side effects: metallic taste, peripheral neuropathy (prolonged use). Reference: KD Tripathi — Metronidazole.

questions.questionText: Amphotericin B is the gold standard antifungal and acts by:
questions.optionB: Binding ergosterol in fungal cell membrane → forming pores → membrane disruption (fungicidal)
questions.explanation: Amphotericin B: polyene antifungal — binds ergosterol (unique to fungal membranes) → creates pores → loss of intracellular contents → cell death. Broadest spectrum antifungal (cryptococcosis, histoplasmosis, mucormycosis, invasive candidiasis, aspergillosis). IV formulation. Toxicities: nephrotoxicity (dose-limiting — causes renal tubular acidosis, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia), infusion reactions (fever, rigors — 'shake and bake'), anemia. Liposomal formulation: less nephrotoxic. Reference: KD Tripathi — Amphotericin B.

questions.questionText: Acyclovir is the drug of choice for:
questions.optionB: HSV (herpes simplex virus) and VZV (varicella-zoster virus) infections
questions.explanation: Acyclovir: guanosine analog, selectively activated by viral thymidine kinase (TK) in HSV/VZV-infected cells → phosphorylated → inhibits viral DNA polymerase + chain termination. Selective toxicity (only active in infected cells). Uses: HSV encephalitis (IV), genital herpes, herpes zoster (shingles). Valacyclovir: prodrug of acyclovir (better oral bioavailability). Resistance: TK mutations (immunocompromised — use foscarnet). Also active against EBV. NOT effective against CMV (lacks TK — use ganciclovir). Reference: KD Tripathi — Acyclovir.

questions.optionB: Sequential blockade of folate synthesis — SMX inhibits dihydropteroate synthase, TMP inhibits dihydrofolate reductase
questions.explanation: TMP-SMX: synergistic combination blocking folate synthesis at two sequential steps. SMX (sulfonamide) → inhibits dihydropteroate synthase (PABA → dihydropteroic acid). TMP → inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHF → THF). Sequential block → synergistic bactericidal effect. Uses: UTI (most common), PCP prophylaxis (HIV — CD4<200), MRSA skin infections, Nocardia, Toxoplasma. Side effects: Steven-Johnson syndrome, bone marrow suppression, crystalluria, hyperkalemia. Reference: KD Tripathi — TMP-SMX.
